title: Foundry SDK Core
summary: Master the Python SDK plumbing for a Microsoft Foundry project: package install, project endpoints, AIProjectClient construction, azure-core patterns, operation groups, connections, sync and async clients, version discipline, telemetry wiring, and service error handling.

concepts.title: The SDK Map
concepts.firstPrinciples: A remote Foundry project is just a set of service resources behind HTTPS. Python cannot touch those resources directly; it needs a library that knows the REST routes, shapes requests, authenticates each call, and turns responses into Python objects.
concepts.definition: The azure-ai-projects package is the Microsoft Foundry SDK package for Python. Its main entry point is azure.ai.projects.AIProjectClient, which gives code-first access to project resources such as agents, connections, deployments, datasets, indexes, evaluation rules, telemetry, and an authenticated OpenAI client.
concepts.analogy: Think of Foundry as a well-guarded workshop. The project endpoint is the workshop address, the credential is your badge, and AIProjectClient is the organized tool chest that knows which drawer holds each project tool.
concepts.mentalModel: Start with package then client then operation group. The package is installed once, the client is constructed per project endpoint, and operation groups are used for focused tasks.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
PythonApp[Python app] --> Package[azure ai projects package]
Package --> Client[AIProjectClient]
Client --> Project[Foundry project]
Project --> Resources[Agents connections deployments datasets indexes]

code.caption: Print the project client type after constructing it from the endpoint.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient

endpoint = os.environ["FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT"]

with DefaultAzureCredential() as credential, AIProjectClient(
    endpoint=endpoint,
    credential=credential,
) as project_client:
    print(type(project_client).__name__)

drills.q: What problem does azure-ai-projects solve?
drills.a: It hides the raw Foundry REST calls behind Python clients, models, paging, errors, and authentication integration.

drills.q: What is the main class a beginner should look for first?
drills.a: AIProjectClient in the azure.ai.projects namespace.

drills.q: Is the package only for inference prompts?
drills.a: No. It is project plumbing: connections, deployments, datasets, indexes, agents, evaluation-related operations, telemetry, and OpenAI client creation.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: The SDK is not the model. It is the Python control panel for a Foundry project. I point AIProjectClient at one project endpoint and then use its operation groups to reach project resources.

glossary.definition: A scoped workspace in Microsoft Foundry containing deployments, connections, datasets, indexes, agents, and related project assets.
The Python client class that targets one Foundry project endpoint with an Azure TokenCredential.
A focused sub-client hanging off AIProjectClient, such as connections or deployments.

sources.title: Azure AI Projects client library for Python
AIProjectClient class reference
azure-ai-projects on PyPI

concepts.title: Install the SDK Stack
concepts.firstPrinciples: A Python process imports exactly the packages available in its environment. If project code depends on Azure SDK packages, those packages must be installed into an isolated environment so your lesson does not depend on whatever happens to be installed globally.
concepts.definition: Use a virtual environment and pip to install azure-ai-projects for Foundry project operations, azure-identity for managed identity and DefaultAzureCredential, and azure-ai-inference when you need the separate preview inference clients. Async project clients also need an async transport such as aiohttp.
concepts.analogy: A venv is a clean field kit. You pack exactly the tools for this mission instead of borrowing random tools from the operating system garage.
concepts.mentalModel: Create .venv, install pinned packages, verify with pip show, and run examples from that activated environment. Keep requirements in source control when building a real course app.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart TD
Venv[Create venv] --> Pip[Install packages]
Pip --> Verify[Verify versions]
Verify --> Run[Run SDK examples]

code.caption: Runnable helper that prints the exact install commands for this lane.
code.content:
commands = [
    "python3 -m venv .venv",
    "source .venv/bin/activate",
    "python -m pip install --upgrade pip",
    "python -m pip install azure-ai-projects azure-identity azure-ai-inference",
    "python -m pip show azure-ai-projects azure-identity azure-ai-inference",
]
for command in commands:
    print(command)

drills.q: Why use a venv?
drills.a: To isolate package versions so examples are reproducible and do not depend on global Python state.

drills.q: Which package contains AIProjectClient?
drills.a: azure-ai-projects.

drills.q: Which package supplies DefaultAzureCredential and ManagedIdentityCredential?
drills.a: azure-identity.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: Before writing SDK code, I create a clean Python environment. I install azure-ai-projects for the project client, azure-identity for token credentials, and azure-ai-inference only when I need the separate inference-preview clients. Then I verify versions before debugging code.

glossary.definition: Python's built-in virtual environment mechanism for isolated package installs.
The standard Python package installer used to install Azure SDK libraries into the active environment.
A separate preview client library for model inference clients such as chat completions and embeddings.

sources.title: Azure AI Projects client library for Python

Usage patterns with the Azure libraries for Python
azure-ai-projects on PyPI

concepts.title: Project Endpoint as Address
concepts.firstPrinciples: Every network client needs a destination. In Foundry, the destination is not a vague account name; it is a project-scoped URL. That URL lets the SDK route calls to the correct project boundary.
concepts.definition: The current docs show a Foundry project endpoint in the form https://your-ai-services-account-name.services.ai.azure.com/api/projects/your-project-name. Store it in FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT and read it from the environment rather than hard-coding it.
concepts.analogy: The endpoint is the GPS coordinate for one project. If the credential is your badge, the endpoint is the building and room number where the badge should be presented.
concepts.mentalModel: One endpoint maps one client to one Foundry project. Change the environment variable to target another project without changing code.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
Env[FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT] --> Client[AIProjectClient]
Client --> Account[AI services account]
Account --> Project[Project path]

code.caption: Validate endpoint shape before creating the client.
code.content:
import os
from urllib.parse import urlparse

endpoint = os.environ.get("FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT", "")
parsed = urlparse(endpoint)

if parsed.scheme != "https" or "/api/projects/" not in parsed.path:
    raise SystemExit("Set FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT to your Foundry project URL")

print("Endpoint looks project scoped")
print(parsed.netloc)
print(parsed.path)

drills.q: Why not put the endpoint literal in code?
drills.a: Environment configuration lets the same code run in dev, test, and production without edits.

drills.q: What does the /api/projects segment communicate?
drills.a: The URL is project scoped, not just account scoped.

drills.q: What changes when you switch projects?
drills.a: Usually only the endpoint environment variable changes.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: The endpoint is the address of the Foundry project I want to operate on. I keep it in FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT so configuration stays outside code. AIProjectClient uses that address for every operation group under the client.

glossary.definition: The HTTPS URL a client uses as the base destination for service calls.
The practice of storing deployment-specific configuration in the environment, not in source code.
Targeting a specific Foundry project path rather than an entire tenant or account.

sources.title: Azure AI Projects client library for Python
AIProjectClient class reference
Usage patterns with the Azure libraries for Python

concepts.title: Construct AIProjectClient
concepts.firstPrinciples: An SDK client is a durable object that holds endpoint, credential, pipeline policies, and transport. Constructing it correctly is the difference between random HTTP calls and a reusable project gateway.
concepts.definition: AIProjectClient is constructed as AIProjectClient(endpoint: str, credential: TokenCredential, *, allow_preview: bool = False, **kwargs). Use it as a context manager so underlying transports close cleanly. Authentication is represented only as a TokenCredential object such as DefaultAzureCredential or ManagedIdentityCredential.
concepts.analogy: The client is a mission radio tuned to one frequency. The endpoint tunes the radio; the credential proves who is speaking; the context manager turns the radio off when done.
concepts.mentalModel: Create credential, create client, call operation groups, leave the with block. For preview features, opt in deliberately with allow_preview=True instead of accidentally relying on unstable behavior.
concepts.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
participant App
participant Credential
participant Client
participant Foundry
App->>Credential: request token
App->>Client: endpoint plus credential
Client->>Foundry: authorized HTTPS calls
Foundry-->>Client: project resources

code.caption: Construct the synchronous client and list available high-level attributes safely.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient

endpoint = os.environ["FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT"]

with DefaultAzureCredential() as credential:
    with AIProjectClient(endpoint=endpoint, credential=credential) as project_client:
        names = ["agents", "connections", "datasets", "deployments", "indexes", "evaluation_rules", "beta"]
        for name in names:
            print(name, hasattr(project_client, name))

drills.q: What two required constructor inputs does AIProjectClient need?
drills.a: endpoint and credential.

drills.q: Why use with AIProjectClient as project_client?
drills.a: It ensures the client closes its underlying resources when the block exits.

drills.q: What does allow_preview do?
drills.a: It deliberately enables certain preview features on the client; beta sub-client methods are preview by name.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: I build AIProjectClient with one project endpoint and one TokenCredential. The client owns the HTTP pipeline and exposes project operation groups. I use a context manager and only set allow_preview when I intentionally need preview features.

glossary.definition: The azure-core credential protocol used by Azure SDK clients to obtain bearer tokens.
A Python with block pattern that opens and closes resources deterministically.
A constructor flag that opts in to selected preview Foundry features.

sources.title: AIProjectClient class reference
Azure Identity client library for Python
Azure AI Projects client library for Python

concepts.title: Azure Core Plumbing
concepts.firstPrinciples: Every Azure SDK call becomes an HTTP request. Cross-cutting mechanics such as retries, logging, transport, tracing hooks, headers, and exceptions should be consistent across packages; otherwise each library would behave differently under failure.
concepts.definition: azure-core is the shared foundation used by Azure SDK client libraries. It supplies common pipeline behavior, transports, exceptions such as HttpResponseError, configuration kwargs such as logging_enable and retry settings, paging types, and tracing integration points.
concepts.analogy: azure-core is the road system under every Azure SDK vehicle. Each service client is a different vehicle, but they all use the same roads, traffic rules, retry lanes, and emergency signals.
concepts.mentalModel: Think request pipeline: your method call enters policies, gains auth and headers, travels through a transport, and returns a response or an AzureError-derived exception.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
MethodCall[SDK method call] --> Policies[azure core policies]
Policies --> Transport[HTTP transport]
Transport --> Service[Foundry service]
Service --> Response[model or HttpResponseError]

code.caption: Enable Azure SDK logging at the client level without exposing secrets in code.
code.content:
import logging
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)

with DefaultAzureCredential() as credential, AIProjectClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=credential,
    logging_enable=True,
) as project_client:
    print("Client logging is wired. Avoid DEBUG logs in shared outputs.")

drills.q: Why does azure-core matter if I only import azure.ai.projects?
drills.a: azure-ai-projects builds on azure-core for the pipeline, transport, paging, tracing, and exceptions.

drills.q: What does transport mean?
drills.a: The component that actually sends HTTP requests and receives responses.

drills.q: Why be careful with logging?
drills.a: Logs can contain request and response details, so avoid sharing verbose logs without review.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: azure-core is the shared engine room beneath the Foundry SDK. It handles HTTP pipeline behavior like retries, logging, transport, tracing hooks, and standard exceptions. When I see HttpResponseError or ItemPaged, I am seeing azure-core concepts.

glossary.definition: The ordered chain of policies that prepares, sends, retries, logs, traces, and processes an SDK HTTP request.
The HTTP implementation used by the pipeline to send requests.
The base exception type for Azure SDK failures.

sources.title: Azure Core shared client library for Python
HttpResponseError class reference
Usage patterns with the Azure libraries for Python

concepts.title: Operation Groups Current Map
concepts.firstPrinciples: A big client becomes learnable when related operations are grouped. Instead of one giant method list, AIProjectClient exposes named operation groups that match project resource families.
concepts.definition: Current GA reference lists AIProjectClient variables beta, agents, evaluation_rules, connections, datasets, deployments, and indexes. The README also describes OpenAI client access with get_openai_client and evaluation tools through evaluation_rules plus beta evaluation sub-clients. The older or requested names .inference and .evaluations are not listed in the current GA AIProjectClient reference, so treat them as version-sensitive until the exact installed package confirms them.
concepts.analogy: The client is a command center with labeled desks. Agents sit at one desk, connections at another, deployments at another. If a desk was renamed between previews, read the map for the version you installed before giving orders.
concepts.mentalModel: Use dir or hasattr for exploration, but rely on the official reference and changelog for course material. Teach the stable names first and mark preview or renamed surfaces clearly.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart TD
Client[AIProjectClient] --> Agents[agents]
Client --> Connections[connections]
Client --> Deployments[deployments]
Client --> Datasets[datasets]
Client --> Indexes[indexes]
Client --> EvalRules[evaluation rules]
Client --> Beta[beta preview groups]
Client --> OpenAI[get openai client]

code.caption: Show the learner how to inspect operation groups without calling the service.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient

expected = [
    "agents", "connections", "datasets", "deployments",
    "indexes", "evaluation_rules", "beta", "telemetry",
    "inference", "evaluations",
]

with DefaultAzureCredential() as credential, AIProjectClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=credential,
) as project_client:
    for name in expected:
        print(f"{name}: {hasattr(project_client, name)}")

drills.q: Which current GA operation groups are clearly listed in the AIProjectClient reference?
drills.a: agents, connections, datasets, deployments, indexes, evaluation_rules, beta, plus methods such as get_openai_client.

drills.q: What should you do if a tutorial uses project_client.inference?
drills.a: Check the installed azure-ai-projects version and current reference; that surface appears version-sensitive and is not in the current GA reference I verified.

drills.q: Where do preview operation groups tend to live?
drills.a: Under project_client.beta or behind explicit preview opt-in depending on the feature.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: Operation groups are the client’s labeled departments. Current GA docs list agents, connections, datasets, deployments, indexes, evaluation_rules, and beta; OpenAI access comes through get_openai_client. If I see .inference or .evaluations in older material, I verify the installed SDK and mark it as version-sensitive.

glossary.definition: Operation group for creating and managing Foundry agents and agent versions at a high level.
Operation group for enumerating and retrieving project connections to external or Azure resources.
Operation group for enumerating AI models deployed to the Foundry project.
Current GA operation group for evaluation rule operations; broader evaluator tools may appear under beta surfaces.

Azure AI Projects client library for Python
azure-ai-projects changelog

concepts.title: Get an OpenAI Client from the Project
concepts.firstPrinciples: Model calls still need a network endpoint and authorization. If the project client already knows the project endpoint and credential, it can create a model-facing OpenAI client that inherits the same project context instead of making you rewire auth separately.
concepts.definition: AIProjectClient.get_openai_client returns an authenticated OpenAI client from the openai package. The reference says it appends /openai/v1 to the project endpoint and wires authentication to a bearer token provider using the AIProjectClient credential with scope https://ai.azure.com/.default. This is the current project-first path for Responses, Conversations, Evaluations, Files, and Fine-Tuning operations.
concepts.analogy: The project client is a secure hotel concierge. You do not ask for a second key at every room; the concierge creates the right room-specific pass for the OpenAI-compatible hallway.
concepts.mentalModel: Construct project_client once, call get_openai_client inside the context, and pass the model deployment name from the environment. This lane shows plumbing only; prompt design and inference behavior belong elsewhere.
concepts.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
participant App
participant ProjectClient
participant OpenAIClient
participant FoundryOpenAI
App->>ProjectClient: get openai client
ProjectClient->>OpenAIClient: base url and token provider
App->>OpenAIClient: responses create
OpenAIClient->>FoundryOpenAI: authorized model call

code.caption: Get an OpenAI client and print its class without sending a prompt.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient

with DefaultAzureCredential() as credential, AIProjectClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=credential,
) as project_client:
    with project_client.get_openai_client() as openai_client:
        print(type(openai_client).__name__)
        print("Ready to call Responses with FOUNDRY_MODEL_NAME when needed")

drills.q: What method creates the OpenAI-compatible client from AIProjectClient?
drills.a: get_openai_client.

drills.q: Why is this better than manually rebuilding auth?
drills.a: The project client passes its TokenCredential through a bearer token provider for the OpenAI client.

drills.q: What environment variable commonly holds the deployment name in current samples?
drills.a: FOUNDRY_MODEL_NAME.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: For model-facing calls from a Foundry project, I can ask AIProjectClient for an authenticated OpenAI client. The project endpoint becomes the OpenAI base URL with /openai/v1 appended. The same Azure credential flows through as a bearer token provider, so no secrets are placed in code.

glossary.definition: The client from the openai package returned by AIProjectClient for OpenAI-compatible operations against the Foundry project.
The root URL used by the OpenAI client, derived from the Foundry project endpoint.
A callable that supplies access tokens to the OpenAI client instead of a static key.

sources.title: AIProjectClient class reference
Azure AI Projects client library for Python
azure-ai-projects changelog

concepts.title: Azure AI Inference Boundary
concepts.firstPrinciples: Two clients can both talk to AI services but serve different layers. One may manage project plumbing; another may target model inference endpoints directly. Confusing the layers creates brittle examples.
concepts.definition: azure-ai-inference is a separate preview package with ChatCompletionsClient, EmbeddingsClient, and load_client for model inference endpoints. Current azure-ai-projects GA documentation emphasizes get_openai_client from AIProjectClient rather than a GA .inference operation group. Therefore, teach azure-ai-inference as a neighboring package and mark project_client.inference examples as version-sensitive unless the learner's installed package exposes them.
concepts.analogy: Foundry project plumbing is the airport terminal. azure-ai-inference is a direct gate to a particular runway. Sometimes you go through the terminal concierge; sometimes you target a gate directly, but they are not the same object.
concepts.mentalModel: Install azure-ai-inference when comparing direct inference clients, but for project-scoped OpenAI access use AIProjectClient.get_openai_client in current GA examples.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
ProjectPath[Project scoped path] --> AIProjectClient
AIProjectClient --> OpenAIClient[get openai client]
DirectEndpoint[Model endpoint] --> InferenceClients[ChatCompletionsClient and EmbeddingsClient]

code.caption: Import the inference client classes without making a service call.
code.content:
from azure.ai.inference import ChatCompletionsClient, EmbeddingsClient, load_client

print(ChatCompletionsClient.__name__)
print(EmbeddingsClient.__name__)
print(load_client.__name__)
print("Use these with direct model endpoints, not as a substitute for AIProjectClient plumbing.")

drills.q: Is azure-ai-inference the same package as azure-ai-projects?
drills.a: No. It is a separate preview inference library.

drills.q: What direct inference clients does the inference README name?
drills.a: ChatCompletionsClient and EmbeddingsClient.

drills.q: What is the current project-scoped path for an OpenAI-compatible client?
drills.a: AIProjectClient.get_openai_client.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: azure-ai-projects is the Foundry project plumbing package. azure-ai-inference is a separate preview package for direct model inference clients. If I see project_client.inference in older lessons, I verify the exact SDK version before teaching it as current.

glossary.definition: An azure-ai-inference client for chat completions against supported inference endpoints.
An azure-ai-inference client for embedding requests against supported inference endpoints.
A package or feature stage that may change and is not always recommended for production workloads.

sources.title: Azure AI Inference client library for Python
Azure AI Projects client library for Python
AIProjectClient class reference

concepts.title: Connections via SDK
concepts.firstPrinciples: Project code often needs other resources: storage, search, OpenAI, Cosmos DB, App Insights, or custom resources. A connection records where that resource is and how the Foundry project knows it, so SDK features can discover resources without hard-coding every target.
concepts.definition: project_client.connections exposes list, get, and get_default. list returns ItemPaged of Connection. Connection fields include name, id, type, target, is_default, credentials, and metadata. Known connection types include AzureOpenAI, AzureBlob, AzureStorageAccount, CognitiveSearch, CosmosDB, ApiKey, AppConfig, AppInsights, CustomKeys, and RemoteTool_Preview.
concepts.analogy: Connections are the project’s address book. Instead of memorizing every warehouse and lab address, the SDK asks the address book for the named or default resource.
concepts.mentalModel: List connections for discovery, get one by name for explicit wiring, or get_default by type when the project owner has marked a standard resource. Avoid include_credentials unless a specialized scenario truly requires it.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart TD
Project[Foundry project] --> Connections[connections group]
Connections --> List[list returns ItemPaged]
Connections --> Get[get by name]
Connections --> Default[get default by type]
List --> Connection[Connection name type target]

code.caption: List connection names, types, and targets.

drills.q: What does project_client.connections.list return?
drills.a: An azure-core ItemPaged iterator of Connection objects.

drills.q: What is a connection target?
drills.a: The URL or target endpoint for the connected resource.

drills.q: Why do connections matter?
drills.a: They let SDK features discover project-linked resources without scattering resource URLs through code.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: A Foundry connection is the project address book entry for another resource. The SDK can list connections, get one by name, or ask for the default connection type. Each Connection tells me its name, type, target, default status, metadata, and credential shape.

glossary.definition: A Foundry project resource describing a linked service target and its connection metadata.
A connections operation that returns the default connection for a specified type.
An optional flag on get operations that asks the service to include credential details in the response.

sources.title: ConnectionsOperations class reference
Connection model reference
ItemPaged class reference

concepts.title: Paging and Long Running Operations
concepts.firstPrinciples: Cloud services protect themselves and clients by splitting large result sets into pages and by turning slow operations into pollable jobs. The SDK makes both patterns feel Pythonic, but the mental model matters for cost, latency, and retries.
concepts.definition: ItemPaged is azure-core's iterator-like paging type; you can iterate item by item or by_page with continuation tokens. Long-running operations in azure-core-based libraries typically use begin_ methods returning pollers whose result method waits for completion. In this lane, know the pattern; deep operation-specific LROs belong to the feature lanes that create resources.
concepts.analogy: Paging is reading a thick dossier one folder at a time. A long-running operation is dropping off a work order, taking a receipt, and checking back until the shop says the job is done.
concepts.mentalModel: For list operations, stream results and stop when you have enough. For begin_ operations, keep the poller and call result only when you are ready to wait.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
ListCall[list call] --> Page1[page one]
Page1 --> Page2[page two]
BeginCall[begin operation] --> Poller[poller]
Poller --> Result[result]

code.caption: Iterate connection results by page to make paging visible.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient

with DefaultAzureCredential() as credential, AIProjectClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=credential,
) as project_client:
    paged = project_client.connections.list()
    for page_number, page in enumerate(paged.by_page(), start=1):
        print(f"Page {page_number}")
        for connection in page:
            print(" ", connection.name)
        break

drills.q: Why do list operations page results?
drills.a: To avoid returning huge responses and to let clients stream through results safely.

drills.q: How do you inspect pages explicitly?
drills.a: Call by_page on an ItemPaged result.

drills.q: What naming pattern often marks a long-running operation in azure-core-based libraries?
drills.a: A begin_ prefix returning a poller.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: Azure SDK lists often return ItemPaged, so I can iterate naturally or page by page. Long operations often return a poller from a begin_ method and I call result when I want to wait. These are azure-core patterns I will meet across Foundry SDK work.

glossary.definition: An iterator-like azure-core type for paged service results.
An opaque token that tells the service where to continue listing results.
An object representing a long-running operation that can be waited on for a final result.

sources.title: ItemPaged class reference
Usage patterns with the Azure libraries for Python
Azure Core shared client library for Python

concepts.title: Sync and Async Clients
concepts.firstPrinciples: Concurrency is about waiting efficiently. A script can block while one request finishes, but a web service or batch worker may need to keep doing other work while many network calls are in flight.
concepts.definition: The synchronous client is azure.ai.projects.AIProjectClient with azure.identity.DefaultAzureCredential. The asynchronous client is azure.ai.projects.aio.AIProjectClient with azure.identity.aio.DefaultAzureCredential and async with. The async reference returns AsyncOpenAI from get_openai_client, and async usage needs an async transport such as aiohttp.
concepts.analogy: Sync is one clerk walking each form to the counter. Async is one dispatcher tracking many forms while the counters work in parallel.
concepts.mentalModel: Use sync for notebooks, CLIs, and simple lessons. Use async when your app already runs asyncio or needs many concurrent network operations.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart TD
SimpleScript[Simple script] --> SyncClient[azure ai projects AIProjectClient]
AsyncApp[Async app] --> AioClient[azure ai projects aio AIProjectClient]
AioClient --> AsyncCredential[azure identity aio credential]
AioClient --> AsyncOpenAI[AsyncOpenAI client]

code.caption: Construct the async project client and print its type.
code.content:
import asyncio
import os
from azure.ai.projects.aio import AIProjectClient
from azure.identity.aio import DefaultAzureCredential

async def main():
    async with DefaultAzureCredential() as credential:
        async with AIProjectClient(
            endpoint=os.environ["FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT"],
            credential=credential,
        ) as project_client:
            print(type(project_client).__name__)

asyncio.run(main())

drills.q: What namespace holds the async project client?
drills.a: azure.ai.projects.aio.

drills.q: What keyword manages async clients?
drills.a: async with.

drills.q: When should a beginner prefer sync?
drills.a: For simple scripts, notebooks, and lessons where concurrency is not the main topic.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: Sync clients block until each network call returns. Async clients use the aio namespace and async with so my program can wait on network work efficiently. I choose async because of my app shape, not because it is magically more correct.

glossary.definition: Azure SDK namespace convention for asynchronous clients and credentials.
Python syntax for asynchronously opening and closing resources.
The async OpenAI client returned by the async AIProjectClient get_openai_client method.

sources.title: Async AIProjectClient class reference

Usage patterns with the Azure libraries for Python
Azure Identity client library for Python

concepts.title: Configuration and Versioning Discipline
concepts.firstPrinciples: A learning site rots when code examples silently drift from the installed SDK. Versioning discipline turns moving cloud APIs into reproducible lessons.
concepts.definition: Read endpoint and deployment names from environment variables such as FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT and FOUNDRY_MODEL_NAME. Pin package versions in requirements files for course runs. Use the PyPI page to see releases and the Azure SDK for Python changelog to detect GA versus preview changes, renamed environment variables, and breaking changes.
concepts.analogy: Version pinning is freezing the training range before a live-fire exercise. Everyone fires at the same targets with the same equipment, so misses teach skill rather than chaos.
concepts.mentalModel: Use constraints like azure-ai-projects>=2.2.0,<3.0.0 for a course cohort, then intentionally review and update the changelog before widening versions.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
EnvVars[Environment variables] --> Code[Same code]
PinnedReq[requirements pin] --> Install[Repeatable install]
Changelog[Changelog review] --> Upgrade[Intentional upgrade]

code.caption: Parse required configuration and show a sample requirements set.
code.content:
import os

required = ["FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT"]
missing = [name for name in required if not os.environ.get(name)]
if missing:
    raise SystemExit(f"Missing config: {', '.join(missing)}")

requirements = [
    "azure-ai-projects>=2.2.0,<3.0.0",
    "azure-identity>=1.25.0,<2.0.0",
    "azure-ai-inference>=1.0.0b9,<2.0.0",
]
print("Suggested course constraints:")
print("\n".join(requirements))

drills.q: Why pin versions for lessons?
drills.a: So every learner sees the same API behavior while learning.

drills.q: Where do you check SDK release changes?
drills.a: The Azure SDK for Python changelog and PyPI release history.

drills.q: Why store FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT in the environment?
drills.a: It keeps deployment-specific configuration out of source code.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: Configuration belongs in environment variables, not literals. SDK versions belong in a pinned requirements file when teaching or deploying. Before upgrading, I read the changelog because preview and GA surfaces can rename methods, environment variables, and operation groups.

glossary.definition: Restricting installed package versions to a known compatible range.
Generally available release stage intended for production use under normal support expectations.
A release history file describing added features, fixes, and breaking changes.

sources.title: azure-ai-projects on PyPI

concepts.title: Tracing and Observability Wiring
concepts.firstPrinciples: You cannot operate what you cannot see. SDK plumbing needs visibility into latency, failures, and request paths, but tracing must be wired deliberately because telemetry can contain sensitive data.
concepts.definition: Current docs describe Foundry tracing through Application Insights and OpenTelemetry. AIProjectClient exposes a telemetry operation with get_application_insights_connection_string. Azure Monitor OpenTelemetry Distro provides configure_azure_monitor, and azure-core tracing can be enabled with OpenTelemetry packages. Some docs and samples discuss tracing as preview or experimental for GenAI spans, so mark detailed span semantics as unstable.
concepts.analogy: Telemetry is the aircraft black box. It should record enough to investigate turbulence, but you must protect what it records and who can read it.
concepts.mentalModel: First connect Application Insights to the Foundry project. Then retrieve or provide the connection string through configuration, call configure_azure_monitor, and run SDK calls with tracing packages installed. Do not print or commit telemetry connection strings in real apps.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
FoundryProject[Foundry project] --> AppInsights[Application Insights]
PythonApp[Python app] --> OpenTelemetry[OpenTelemetry]
OpenTelemetry --> AzureMonitor[Azure Monitor]
SDKCalls[SDK calls] --> OpenTelemetry

code.caption: Wire Azure Monitor using the connection string from environment.
code.content:
import os
from azure.monitor.opentelemetry import configure_azure_monitor

connection_string = os.environ.get("APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING")
if not connection_string:
    raise SystemExit("Set APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING from your monitored environment")

configure_azure_monitor(connection_string=connection_string)
print("Azure Monitor OpenTelemetry wiring is configured")

drills.q: What project operation can retrieve the Application Insights connection string?
drills.a: project_client.telemetry.get_application_insights_connection_string.

drills.q: What package provides configure_azure_monitor?
drills.a: azure-monitor-opentelemetry.

drills.q: Why be cautious with traces?
drills.a: They can capture prompts, outputs, tool arguments, latency, exceptions, and other sensitive operational data.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: Observability is wiring, not magic. Foundry can connect to Application Insights, Python can use OpenTelemetry, and Azure Monitor can collect the spans. I treat detailed GenAI tracing semantics as evolving and protect telemetry like production logs.

glossary.definition: An open standard for traces, metrics, and logs across applications and services.
Azure Monitor application performance and telemetry resource used to store and query traces.
Operational data such as spans, latency, exceptions, logs, and metrics emitted by an application or service.

concepts.title: HttpResponseError Patterns
concepts.firstPrinciples: Networked code fails in layers: configuration can be missing, credentials can fail, permissions can be insufficient, resources can be absent, and services can throttle. Robust SDK code separates expected service failures from programmer mistakes.
concepts.definition: Azure SDK client methods that make service calls raise azure.core.exceptions.HttpResponseError for non-success HTTP responses. The exception exposes status_code, reason, message, response, and sometimes structured error data. Handle it around the smallest operation that can fail usefully.
concepts.analogy: HttpResponseError is the service returning a stamped rejection form rather than the mail truck crashing. The form has a status code and reason that tell you which counter rejected the request.
concepts.mentalModel: Catch HttpResponseError, branch on status_code for known cases such as 401, 403, 404, or 429, log safe details, and re-raise when the program cannot recover.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart TD
SDKCall[SDK call] --> Success[Return model]
SDKCall --> Failure[HttpResponseError]
Failure --> Status[Inspect status code]
Status --> Recover[Recover or re raise]

code.caption: Handle service errors when listing deployments.

drills.q: Which exception should wrap non-success service responses?
drills.a: azure.core.exceptions.HttpResponseError.

drills.q: What fields are useful on HttpResponseError?
drills.a: status_code, reason, message, response, and structured error when available.

drills.q: Should you catch every exception and continue?
drills.a: No. Recover from known cases and re-raise unknown failures.

concepts.teachBack: Teach it back as: SDK service calls can fail with HttpResponseError when the HTTP response is not successful. I inspect status_code and reason to decide whether to recover, retry, or re-raise. I keep the try block tight so errors are handled near the operation that caused them.

glossary.definition: Azure-core exception raised when a service returns a non-success HTTP status code.
The numeric HTTP status associated with a failed service response.
Raising the caught exception again when code cannot safely recover.

quiz.q: Which package provides AIProjectClient for Microsoft Foundry project plumbing?
quiz.explain: AIProjectClient is in azure.ai.projects from the azure-ai-projects package.

quiz.q: What is the current documented constructor shape for the synchronous AIProjectClient?
quiz.explain: The reference shows endpoint and TokenCredential, with allow_preview as an optional keyword-only flag.

quiz.q: Where should a lesson store FOUNDRY_PROJECT_ENDPOINT?
quiz.explain: 12-factor configuration keeps project-specific endpoints outside source code.

quiz.q: Which method returns an authenticated OpenAI client from AIProjectClient?
quiz.explain: Current docs describe AIProjectClient.get_openai_client for OpenAI-compatible operations.

quiz.q: What type does connections.list return according to the reference?
Which namespace contains the async project client?
quiz.explain: Azure SDK async clients commonly live under the aio namespace.

quiz.q: Which exception should you catch for non-success HTTP responses from service calls?
quiz.explain: Azure SDK service calls raise azure.core.exceptions.HttpResponseError for non-success responses.

quiz.q: What is the safest statement about project_client.inference in current teaching material?
options: Verify the installed SDK because current GA AIProjectClient docs do not list it
It is the only supported GA operation group
It stores static secrets in the project
quiz.explain: Current verified GA docs emphasize get_openai_client and do not list .inference as an AIProjectClient variable.
